coredata.dc:title: Electrodeposited pectin/reduced carbon dots scaffold on the pencil graphite electrode as a support of electroloaded nickel nanoparticles for electrocatalytic purpose

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging nanomaterial, have shown significant promise in the materials science. This study aims to electrochemical deposited reduce carbon dots (RCDs) and pectin (PC) scaffold on the pencil graphite electrode (PGE), resulting a multifunctional electrocatalyst support (PC/RCDs/PGE) for electroloading of the nickel nanoparticles (NiNPs) to creates the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE for electrooxidation of ethanol. Comprehensive surface and structural analysis and chemical identification methods were utilized for characterization of the modified electrode. Evaluation as an electrocatalyst for ethanol oxidation in 0.1 M NaOH, demonstrated that the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE shows high electrocatalytic activity (Jp = 112 mA cm−2), outperforming several comparisons modified electrodes, RCDs/PGE, NiNPs/RCDs/PGE, NiNPs/PC/PGE, and NiNPs/PGE. These findings suggest that the RCDs and PC scaffold enhances the electrocatalytic activity of the NiNPs via the increasing the surface area of electrodeposited NiNPs and synergistic effect of nanocomposite components, indicating potential for significant advancements in non-platinum ethanol oxidation electrocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Electrodeposited pectin/reduced carbon dots scaffold on the pencil graphite electrode as a support of electroloaded nickel nanoparticles for electrocatalytic purpose

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging nanomaterial, have shown significant promise in the materials science. This study aims to electrochemical deposited reduce carbon dots (RCDs) and pectin (PC) scaffold on the pencil graphite electrode (PGE), resulting a multifunctional electrocatalyst support (PC/RCDs/PGE) for electroloading of the nickel nanoparticles (NiNPs) to creates the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE for electrooxidation of ethanol. Comprehensive surface and structural analysis and chemical identification methods were utilized for characterization of the modified electrode. Evaluation as an electrocatalyst for ethanol oxidation in 0.1 M NaOH, demonstrated that the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE shows high electrocatalytic activity (Jp = 112 mA cm−2), outperforming several comparisons modified electrodes, RCDs/PGE, NiNPs/RCDs/PGE, NiNPs/PC/PGE, and NiNPs/PGE. These findings suggest that the RCDs and PC scaffold enhances the electrocatalytic activity of the NiNPs via the increasing the surface area of electrodeposited NiNPs and synergistic effect of nanocomposite components, indicating potential for significant advancements in non-platinum ethanol oxidation electrocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing energy needs of our planet are still mainly being fulfilled by oil-based products, highlighting the importance of finding different ways to store energy that will be crucial for future generations [1,2]. One solution gaining traction is fuel cells, which are becoming more popular as a convenient, affordable, and eco-friendly substitute for rechargeable batteries. In particular, direct ethanol fuel cells (DEFCs) present noteworthy benefits due to their superior energy density, unlike methanol [3–5]. The performance of DEFCs is heavily influenced by the activity of electrocatalysts in the ethanol oxidation reaction (EOR). Strong catalytic activity for EOR is indicated by a greater negative onset potential, which characterizes the potential in an electrochemical cell that drives the forward reaction [4,6]. Additionally, high current density at a given potential signifies efficient electrocatalyst activity for EOR, leading to improved performance [7,8]. Currently, the most effective electrocatalysts for EOR are derived from noble metals such as palladium (Pd) or platinum (Pt) and their alloys with transition elements [9,10]. However, their practical application is limited by issues such as CO poisoning of active sites, scarcity, and high costs, which compromise electrocatalyst stability [11]. Consequently, there is a significant scientific focus on developing more stable, affordable, and readily available electrocatalysts for DEFCs [12–14].

Non-noble metal catalysts are becoming more popular because of their affordability, ample availability, and excellent catalytic abilities [9,10,12,15]. They demonstrate potential in addressing the challenges associated with valuable metal catalysts, making them suitable options for upcoming fuel cell technologies [6,16]. Experts are consistently researching and improving these substances to enhance the efficiency and performance [15,17]. Among non-noble metals, nickel stands out for its various energy-related applications, including CO2 reduction [18,19], oxygen reduction reaction [20,21], hydrogen evolution reaction [22,23], methanol oxidation [24–26], ethanol oxidation [27,28] and use in supercapacitors [29,30]. Nickel-based electrocatalysts accelerate electron transfer from the modified electrode to the electrolyte, reduce reaction overpotential, and enhance anodic peak current. Nickel hydroxides have also attracted attention for their remarkable stability in electrochemical applications such as alkaline batteries [31,32], electrochemical sensors [33,34] and fuels oxidation [35–38]. Despite their advantages, the durability of non-noble metal electrocatalysts can be affected by factors such as support material, manufacturing processes, pretreatment, operating environment, and chemical composition. Further research is necessary to promote the commercial feasibility of non-noble metal electrocatalysts in alcohols fuel cells and improve their stability and performance.

The creation of novel, highly stable, and sensitive modified electrodes is crucial for electro-oxidation of fuels. Different carbon substrates can affect the electrochemical stability of metal nanoparticles in fuel cells [39,40]. Carbon dots (CDs), a favorable class of carbon nanomaterials, have attracted interest as promising support for metal nanoparticles due to their high conductivity, good dispersion, modified electronic properties, and excellent optical and electrochemical properties in alcohol fuel cells [41,42]. Recently, carbon dot/polymer nanocomposites have been developed by combining CDs with polymers [3,8,12,26,28,41,43]. These composites offer several advantages, including improved electrochemical performance, increased stability, customized features, and adaptability. The strong contact, π–π stacking, or even hydrogen bonding that underpins this driving force investigation might be used to mix CDs with polymers like pectin to make materials for a variety of uses, including electroanalysis and fuel cells [44,45]. This makes them ideal for applications in energy storage, sensors, and biomedical devices. Pectin (PC) has drawn interest recently due to its prospective uses in a number of industries, including energy storage [46]. Studies have looked at the use of pectin-based nanocomposites in the electrocatalysis [44,47], electrolytes and membranes of fuel cells and batteries [48–50]. The combination of pectin with carbon nanoparticles has shown promising results in improving the performance of these materials in various applications [51,52].

This research highlights a new nanocomposite that consists of nickel nanoparticles (NiNPs) supported by pectin (PC) and reduced carbon dots (RCDs) scaffold on the pencil graphite electrode (PGE); NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE, and serves as an electrocatalyst for EOR in NaOH solution. Electrochemical techniques were employed to produce CDs from ethanol as a carbon origin, electrodeposition of the RCDs and PC scaffold for the first time as a support at the surface of PGE and electroloading of the NiNPs to creates the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE. After comprehensive surface analysis and characterization of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE, the functions of PC and RCDs in the electrocatalytic efficiency and durability of the created electrocatalyst; NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE, were investigated using electrochemical approaches like cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and chronopotentiometry (CP) for EOR in a 0.1 M NaOH solution.

Sigma-Aldrich was the source of the pectin with product code:93854-100G and 50–75% esterification (The molecular mass of pectin depending on its source and level of esterification). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Merck, with at least 99.0% purity), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, Merck, with a quality of 98.0%), nickel sulfate hexahydrate (NiSO4.6H2O, Merck, with a quality of 99.0%), ethanol (C2H5OH, Merck, of exceptionally pure grade), and potassium chloride (KCl, Merck, with at least 99.0% purity) were used without further purification. Deionized water was employed in preparing all mixtures.

The electrochemical experiments were conducted at room temperature using a three-compartment cell. The cell was connected to an Autolab (PGSTAT30) galvanostat/potentiostat, which was controlled by the GPES 4.9 program. For the experiments, the electrodes included a steel rod as the counter electrode, a modified graphite pencil electrode [the Parsikar brand, 3.0 mm 2B, pencil graphite leads (made in P.R.C.) were obtained from local stationery in Urmia/Iran] as the working electrode, and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. This setup was used to perform the all electrochemical studies. To observe the morphology of the modified electrodes, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was conducted. This analysis was performed using a ZEISS company model Sigma VP microscope at an acceleration voltage of 5 kV. Additionally, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of the samples, in the form of KBr pellets, were obtained using a NEXUS 670 FTIR spectrometer. Panalytical Xpert PRO XX-ray powder diffractometer was utilized to analyze the XRD patterns of the produced electrocatalysts. The Cu Ka radiation source utilized had a wavelength of 0.154056 nm, generated at 40 kV and 35 mA. UV–Vis spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu spectrophotometer from Japan. Lastly, the fluorescence spectra of materials were measured using a JASCO FP-750 spectrofluorometer equipped with a 150-W xenon lamp and a 1 cm quartz cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrosynthesis of the CDs and electrodeposition of PC/RCDs at the PGE
ce:section.ce:para: The research involved using a straightforward and cost-efficient electrochemical method to create CDs, RCDs and PC/RCDs. The process of synthesizing of the CDs followed through the procedure described by Liu et al. [53], with some small modifications. More specifically, a setup consisting of two graphite electrodes placed 7 cm apart was utilized. These electrodes were placed in an electrolyte solution made up of 35 mL of ethanol, 8 mL of water, and 0.03 g of NaOH. The system was connected to a direct current power source, with the voltage being set at 5 V for a period of 3 h. During the time of applied potential, the electrochemical reactions of water splitting takes place on the anode and cathode and produced H2 and O2 gases in moderately concentrated salt solutions. However, hydroxyl radicals (OH°) as active intermediates can also be generated on the anode at the high anodic overpotential (e.g., 5 V). The generated hydroxyl radicals corrode the graphite electrode at the edges. In the presence of ethanol, the activity of the hydroxyl radicals moderately controlled and leading to the smooth generation of CDs [54] as a yellow liquid solution of CDs (CDs with hexagonal lattice structure) from electrochemically exfoliated of graphite electrode. As can be seen in Scheme S1 at the Supporting Information (step 1). For electrodeposition of RCDs, firstly, the clean PGE was submerged in the yellow liquid of CDs solution, and its potential was scanned from −1 to 1 V for 25 cycles, using cyclic voltammetry at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 [55]. The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) obtained during the electrodeposition of the RCDs are presented in Fig. S1A at the Supporting Information, which displays distinct redox peaks (a1, c1) in the CV profiles. The peak currents of redox peaks remain consistently stable throughout the sequential scanning, indicating complete deposition under the given conditions and producing of RCDs/PGE (step 2 in Scheme S1 at the Supporting Information). In the next step (step 3), the electrodepositing process of pectin and formation of PC on/in of the RCDs/PGE was performed by cyclic voltammetry method through scanning the potential range at the ranges of −0.6 to 1.1 V in a solution of H2SO4 (0.1 M) + 1 mM pectin. Fig. S1B in the Supporting Information illustrates the progression of CVs of PC deposition on/in RCDS/PGE through cyclic voltammetry. The CV, characterized by dashed line (1st scan with an anodic peak current, a1), illustrates the primary scan of the electrooxidation process of PC on RCDS/PGE. In the second and subsequent CVs, there are two anodic peaks (a2 and a3) and their corresponding cathodic peaks (c2 and c3) during the reverse scan. Subsequent CVs (2–10th scans) illustrate the electrodeposition and developments of PC scaffold on the RCDS/PGE. With an increase in the number of potential cycles, there is a noticeable enhancement in the intensity of anodic and cathodic peaks indicating the formation of a continuous film of PC and a uniformly adhered thick coating on the RCDS/PGE surface. Actually, the acidic environment induces the hydrolysis of PC and facilitates the provision of protons necessary for the electrodeposition process of PC and producing of the PC/RCDs/PGE. A schematic representation of these steps is shown in Scheme S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the modified electrode; NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE
ce:section.ce:para: The modified electrode was prepared using the following methodology. Initially, a copper wire was connected to the opposite end of the PGE to establish an electrical connection with the electrochemical system. It is important to note that the exposed surface area of the electrode in the electrolyte solution should remain consistent throughout all experimental procedures. To ensure this, the lateral surface area of the PGE was covered with a Teflon bar, leaving only the cross-sectional area (3 mm2) of the PGE exposed to the electrolyte solution. Before each experiment, the electrode surface was carefully polished on A4 paper to achieve a smooth and reflective appearance. Due to the relatively softer nature of the PGE surface compared to conventional metal surfaces, the polishing process took approximately 1 min. Afterwards, ultrasonication in deionized water was used to clean the PGE. Then the clean PGE was modified by NiNPs/PC/RCDs through the three discrete electrochemical methods (A schematic representation is shown in Scheme S1); firstly, electrosynthesis of the RCDs at the PGE and then the electrodepositing of the PC on/in the RCDs/PGE to produces the PC/RCDs/PGE (as subsection 2.2) and finally electroloading of the NiNPs on/in support to produces the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE. On the other hand, after electropreparation of the PC/RCDs/PGE, the electrode was removed from the solution, washed, and finally, for the electrodeposition of the NiNPs and construction of NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE, a constant voltage of −1.0 V, vs. SCE, was applied for 300 s in a 0.50 M solution of NiSO4.6H2O + 0.10 M KCl as the electrolyte (step 4 in the Supporting Information). A schematic representation of the modified electrode preparation and its application are shown in Scheme S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the CDs, RCDs and PC/RCDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A shows the spectroscopic behaviors of the electrosynthesised CDs. The graphs in Fig. 1A display both the UV–Visible absorption (Abs) and glow-in-the-dark (PL) of CDs in watery mixture. The UV–vis graph exposes two absorption hills at about 240 and 360 nm. The first hill, which is prominent and clear, corresponds to the π-π∗ shifts of the aromatic CC sp2 bond. The second hill, which blends with the first hill and shows up as a shoulder, is credited to the π-π∗ shifts of the carbonyl functional groups [43,56]. The visual properties of the prepared CDs are further validated by the occurrence of a PL hill at about 480 nm, thus approving their characteristics in the visible light wavelength range when excited at 340 nm [43,56,57]. These behaviors confirm the successfully electrosynthesis of the as-prepared CDs. Also, the FT-IR graph of the prepared CDs (Fig. 1B) verified the occurrence of different types of functional groups. The wide absorption hill of OH at 3500-3600 cm−1, the stretching noise band of CO in –COOH at 1550-1650 cm−1, and the stretching noise bands of C–O at 1000–1100 and 700-800 cm−1 indicate the occurrence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups at the surface of RCDs. Additionally, two noticeable absorption hills at 2900–3100 and 1350-1450 cm−1are connected with the stretch of vibration C–H and CC, corresponding to the occurrence of alkyl and aryl groups [43,56–59]. On the other hand, the surface groups, structure, and components of the PC/RCDs and pure PC were extensively analyzed using FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectrum of pure PC, shown in Fig. 1C, displays unique absorption peaks: 3435 cm−1 assigned for O–H stretching vibration originating from the intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding of the galacturonic acid present in the pectin structure, 2945 cm−1 indicating the stretching vibration of the C–H bond, and 1636 and 1439 cm−1 representing the asymmetrical and symmetrical vibration of the carboxyl group. Additionally, the peaks observed between 1100 and 1200 cm−1 are associated with the presence of R–O–R. Moreover, the FT-IR spectrum ranging from 1300 to 800 cm−1 are categorized as the carbohydrate characteristic region. Notably, the presence of a peak at 776.48 cm−1 is recognized as a characteristic feature of the γ(C–OH) ring [59]. The FT-IR spectrum of the PC/RCDs composite film exhibit absorption bands that are quite similar to those of pure PC [60,61]. Nonetheless, in comparison to pure PC, the spectrum of the PC/RCDs scaffold demonstrate a weaker asymmetrical vibration of the carboxylic group at 1439, along with a broad shoulder near 2600 cm−1 that can be attributed to the O–H stretching vibration in free carboxylate groups (CO–O–H) bonded by hydrogen bonds of dimers in the scaffold. These changes in the FT-IR spectrum indicate that the presence of RCDs in the PC scaffold affects the molecular structure and hydrogen bonding patterns, leading to enhanced stability and mechanical properties in the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the synthesized materials and nanocomposite modified electrode was characterized using FE-SEM images. These images are presented in Fig. 2 : PC/PGE (A), RCDs/PGE (B), PC/RCDs/PGE (C), and NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (D). In Fig. 2, it can be observed that a PC scaffold on PGE results in the formation of string-like fragments on the surface, which consist of individually dispersed particles. Image (B) shows the FE-SEM of RCDs/PGE that indicate the presence of spherical balls of diameter ranging in few hundred nanometers indicating self-assembled mesoscopic structure of much smaller nanoparticles. While image (C) has the surface morphology as image (A), morphology of the PC/PGE. Image (D) shows the entire assembly on the electrode surface (NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE), where the PC scaffold over the layers of RCDs containing metallic nanoparticles.

The Energy dispersive X-ray spectra (EDS) of the synthesized materials and nanocomposite modified electrode were produced and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 3 . Spectra of A, B and C in Fig. 3 display the elemental mapping of C and O atoms associated with the PC/PGE (A), RCDs/PGE (B) and PC/RCDs/PGE (C) which indicate the electrosynthesis of RCDs, PC and PC/RCDs at the surface of PGE. The Au peaks on all EDX spectra correspond to the deposited Au atoms to create electrical conductivity. The EDS of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (spectrum D) exhibits distinct regions of Ni atoms, confirming the electroloading of the NiNPs on the PC/RCDs/PGE. Further insights into the types and distribution of elements present on the surface of the synthesized materials and modified electrodes are provided by the results of elemental mapping, as shown in Fig. 3 (images E-H). As the above spectra, elemental mapping of the PC (E), RCDs (F) and PC/RCDs (G) at the surface of PGE are the same. Moreover, elemental map of H presents the Ni, O, and C atoms corresponding to the electrodeposited NiNPs/PC/RCDs on the surface of the PGE, confirming the uniform distribution of NiNPs on the PC/RCDs/PGE surface.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) technique was used to investigate the crystal phase structure of the synthesized materials and nanocomposite modified electrode. Fig. 4 shows the XRD patterns of the RCDs/PGE, PC/PGE, PC/RCDs/PGE and NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE. As observed, the XRD pattern of the RCDs/PGE shows a peak located at around 2θ = 27.5° which is related to (002) plane of carbon dots [56–58] and a peak at about 2θ = 55.2° corresponds to the (006) plane of graphite in PGE [26,62]. The XRD pattern of the PC/PGE typically exhibits a broad peak in the 2θ ranges of 10–17°. This indicates an amorphous structure of PC and also a peak at about 2θ = 55.2° corresponds to the (006) plane of graphite. In the XRD pattern of the PC/RCDs/PGE, three peaks related to PC (2θ = 10–17°), RCDs (2θ = 27.5°) and 2θ = 55.2° corresponds to PGE are observed. The XRD pattern of the final modified electrode; NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE, additional to the XRD pattern of PC/RCDs/PGE, three prominent peaks are observed at Bragg angle values of 44.4°, 51.8°, and 75.5°. These peaks correspond to the following Ni crystallographic planes: (111), (200), and (202), respectively. This pattern indicates the presence of a face-centered cubic (fcc) structure of NiNPs in the nanocomposite modified electrode. Inset shows the XRD pattern of the PGE, a peak located at around 2θ = 55.2° corresponds to the (006) plane of graphite in PGE.

By analyzing the XRD pattern and main peak in full width at half maximum (FWHM), the average sizes of nanoparticles were determined using the Debye-Scherrer equation [63]:
(1) d = kλB·cos θ
In this study, the average sizes of NiNPs crystallites and RCDs in NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE were determined using the Debye-Scherer equation. The equation includes variables such as grain size (d), FWHM (full width at half maximum) (B) of spherical crystallites with cubic symmetry (k = 0.90–0.94), X-ray wavelength (λ = 0.154056 nm), and the half diffraction angle of the peak (θ). Based on the calculations, the average size of NiNPs and RCDs in the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE were found to be 78.04 nm and 39.01 nm, respectively.

Further investigation of the modified electrodes involved monitoring the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of the modified electrodes in an alkaline solution to confirm the electrodepositing of NiNPs on/in PC/RCDS/PGE. The obtained CVs closely resembles those reported in previous studies [63,64]. Fig. 5 presents the results of the CVs of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (a), NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (c), NiNPs/PGE (d), RCDs/PGE (e) and PGE (f) in NaOH 0.1 M at scan rate of 20 mV s−1. As shown in Fig. 5, the formation of NiNPs on/in the PC/RCDS/PGE surface was confirmed by occurring the anodic and cathodic waves related to the Ni2+/Ni3+ redox couple within the potential ranges of 0.1–0.6 V. Actually, the anodic peak situated at ca. 0.5 V and the related cathodic peak located at ca. 0.30 V are correspond to the oxidation and reduction of Ni(OH)2 to NiOOH and vice versa. While, as can be seen in Fig. 5, there are no redox peak currents on the bare PGE and RCDS/PGE at the scanned potential ranges. In contrast, a pair of strong redox anodic and cathodic peak for all other electrocatalysts (Ni-based modified electrode); NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (CV b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (CV c), NiNPs/PGE (CV d), was observed in the ranges 0.1–0.6 V. In the case of the NiNPs/PC/RCDS/PGE, its electrochemical characteristic is as the same as of the NiNPs/PC/PGE, NiNPs/RCDs/PGE and NiNPs/PGE, except that the CV profile shows a higher anodic and cathodic peaks current density which indicates that the incorporation of the NiNPs on/in the PC/RCDs/PGE and construction of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE significantly enhances its electrochemical performance. The findings strongly suggest that the incorporating of the NiNPs into the structural framework of the PC/RCDs/PGE effectively enhances the electrochemical performance of the NiNPs. Actually, the results clearly show that the using PC/RCDs scaffold with a notable specific surface area is highly beneficial for electroloading of metal nanoparticles; NiNPs. The notable specific surface area of the PC/RCDs scaffold allows the increasing the number of electroactive sites on modified electrode, NiNPs, during the redox transition from Ni2+/Ni3+ in electroreaction process. On the contrary, the NiNPs/PGE (CV d) (the NiNPs without PC/RCDs scaffold) and also NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (CV b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (CV c) don't have such significant improvement in the electrochemical performance of Ni2+/Ni3+ redox couple. These results well indicate that the existence of the PC/RCDs scaffold increased the specific surface area of NiNPs.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of a modified electrode refers to the surface area that is involved in electrochemical reactions. The ECSA can be determined by considering two factors: the double-layer capacitance (Cdl) and the specific capacitance (Cs). The ECSA can be calculated by analyzing the slope of the linear regression that involves the differences in current density within the non-faradaic region of the CV curve. In the case of present modified electrodes, the specific capacitance (Cs) is generally assumed to be 0.04 mF/cm2 and the equation used to calculate the ECSA is: ECSA = Cdl/Cs [65]. According the obtained results, the highest ECSA was calculated for the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE to be as 2.21 cm−2 and the lowest ECSA is 0.94 cm−2 for the NiNPs/PGE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE toward oxidation of ethanol
ce:section.ce:para: In this part, we assessed the electrocatalytic performance of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE toward the oxidation of EOR. For this proposes we carried out experiments on various modified electrodes including NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (a), NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (c), NiNPs/PGE (d), RCDs/PGE (e) and unmodified PGE (f) in the presence of 0.1 M ethanol and the results are depicted Fig. 6. As can be seen, no clear oxidation peak observed for the RCDS/PGE (CV e) and PGE (CV f) which indicates these electrodes have not any electrocatalytic activity toward ethanol oxidation. While others CVs show that the electrocatalytic oxidation of ethanol mainly occurred at the NiNPs-based modified electrodes; NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (CV a), NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (CV b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (CV c) and NiNPs/PGE (CV d). Among the investigated NiNPs-based modified electrodes, NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE exhibited highest electrocatalytic activity and anodic peak current for EOR. The oxidation peak current of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (CV a) approximately 112 mA cm−2 is the highest in the presence of 0.1 M ethanol compared to other Ni-based modified electrodes; NiNPs/RCDS/PGE (CV b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (CV c), NiNPs/PGE (CV d). Practically, the combination of PC/RCDs scaffold provided a large surface area with porous structure which increased the number of NiNPs, electroactive sites of the electrodeposited NiNPs and consequently the high electrocatalytic activity. The NiNPs with a high surface-to-volume ratio on/in the RCDs/PC enhanced their overall surface area and increased the chances of fuel contact and oxidation current. On the other hand, highest anodic peak current in the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE can be attributed to the synergistic effect of the of RCDS, PC and NiNPs in promoting the oxidation of MOR. In the presence ethanol, the anodic peak current associated with the oxidation of Ni3+ species rose, while the cathodic current lowered during the electrocatalytic oxidation of ethanol. This procedure involves the oxidation of Ni2+ species to Ni3+, followed by the creation of Ni3+ species-oxidized ethanol molecules and the restoration of Ni2+ species. The increased positive peak current linked to the oxidation of Ni3+ species in ethanol suggests effective electrocatalytic oxidation. As predicted, the corresponding opposite currents decreased, proving that ethanol experiences cyclic redox reactions facilitated by active Ni3+species during oxidation. The research conducted by previous studies [26,33–35,55,63] has provided significant evidence to support the claim that the observed behavior is caused by an electrochemical reaction undergoes a chemical reaction (EC'), as seen in Scheme 1

The electrochemical step (E) involves the oxidation of Ni(OH)₂ (Ni2+) to NiOOH (Ni3+), while the chemical step (C′) involves the oxidation of ethanol by NiOOH, followed by the regeneration of Ni(OH)₂. Additionally, the electrochemical oxidation of ethanol showed lowest onset potential for the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE in compared with other electrocatalysts as can be seen in Fig. 6 and Table 1 . The gradual increase in anodic peak current and decrease in onset potential further confirm the additive contributions of all three components (RCDs, PC and NiNPs) in the present modified electrode. Tafel polarization curves then were used to investigate the electrocatalytic activities of present electrocatalysts for the MOR. The kinetic parameters for MOR were determined as follows [50].
(2) η = RT/αnF Ln j/j°
In equation (2), the universal gas constant (R), the Faraday constant (F), the temperature (T), the charge transfer coefficient of the reaction (α), and the exchange current density (J0) are all constants. Fig. 7 depicts the Tafel plots from the CVs obtained for a 0.1 M ethanol solution in a 0.1 M NaOH solution on various modified electrodes: NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (a), NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (c) and NiNPs/PGE (d). The Tafel plots were obtained at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. The slopes of the Tafel plots for ethanol electrooxidation were measured as follows: 257 mV dec−1 for NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (a), 273 mV dec−1 for NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (b), 351 mV dec−1 for NiNPs/PC/PGE (c) and 393 mV dec−1 for NiNPs/PGE (d). From NiNPs/PGE to NiNPs/PC/RCDS/PGE, the slope decreased from 393 mV dec−1 to 257 mV dec−1. This suggests that the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE exhibits a lower Tafel slope, reduced overpotential, and enhanced electrocatalytic activity. Table 1 summarizes the experimentally determined kinetic parameters, clearly demonstrating the superior electrocatalytic activity and kinetics of the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE compared to the other electrocatalysts; NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (b), NiNPs/PC/PGE (c) and NiNPs/PGE (d).

To further investigating of the kinetic characteristics, the CVs of the main electrocatalyst; NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE, were recorded at different potential scan rates (20–90 mV s−1) in a 0.1 M NaOH solution containing 0.1 M ethanol. In ethanol electrooxidation [Fig. 8 A], increasing the scan rate led to a steady increase in the anodic peak current densities indicating low kinetic restrictions in the electrocatalytic oxidation processes at the surface of NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE [66]. These results suggest that the increasing of scan rate during the electrooxidation of ethanol gradually increases the anodic peak current densities. This phenomenon has been observed in various electrocatalytic systems and can be attributed to different factors, including the kinetics of electrooxidation reactions and the behavior of the active species involved. The correlation between scan rate and anodic peak current densities is often expressed as directly proportional to the square root of the scan rate, indicating a diffusion-controlled process [67,68]. These findings improve our understanding of the kinetic characteristics of ethanol electrooxidation and the performance of NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE electrocatalyst in this specific process. Fig. 8B shows the CVs of NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE electrocatalyst at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1, with varying concentrations of ethanol. It is observed that the anodic peak current densities increase noticeably as the ethanol concentration rises, until it reaches a concentration of 3.0 M. However, electrocatalysis becomes more challenging above a concentration of 3.0 M. Furthermore, there is no significant further increase in the anodic peak current beyond a concentration of 3.0 M. This phenomenon can be explained by the saturation of active NiNPs active sites on the modified electrode surface, as previously reported [69].

Fig. 9B illustrates the chronopotentiometry (CP) curves for the electrocatalysts under a constant current density of 20 mA cm−2 in 0.1 M NaOH containing 0.1 M ethanol. It is obvious that the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE electrocatalyst (CP gram a) has the minimum potential for the electrooxidation of ethanol compared to the NiNPs/PC/PGE (CP gram b), NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (CP gram c) and NiNPs/PGE (CP gram d). A lower polarization potential indicates improved electrocatalytic performance and decreased vulnerability to poisoning by the electrocatalyst. On the other hand, a rise in polarization potential over time indicates increased poisoning by the electrocatalysts, which results in restricted access to the electrocatalytic sites due to the adsorption of by-product species on the electrocatalyst surface [68]. Importantly, all electrocatalysts show a gradual increase in polarization potential followed by a sudden rise, indicating a loss of activity for electrooxidation of ethanol and the start of the oxygen evolution reaction. During the CP experiment, the surface of the Ni-based electrocatalysts becomes contaminated with poisonous species, especially COads, generated during EOR. This contamination accumulates and hinders further ethanol oxidation. To maintain the applied anodic current density, the potential must be increased to a higher value at which H2O is decomposed [77,78]. Based on the obtained results, the prepared electrocatalysts exhibit different degrees of tolerance to poisoning during the EOR, with the order being NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE (a) > NiNPs/RCDs/PGE (b) > NiNPs/PC/PGE (c) > NiNPs/PGE (d). In other words, the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE electrocatalyst demonstrates good durability and a high tolerance towards poisoning species.

These findings align with and expand upon existing literature, which has demonstrated the potential of non-noble metal electrocatalysts in fuel cell applications. Our study builds on this knowledge by integrating RCDs and PC scaffold to further enhance the electrocatalytic activities of the NiNPs; a simple method was developed for the fabrication of a new nanocomposite on the PGE. The RCDs were firstly prepared at the PGE through the reduction of the electrosynthesised CDs from ethanol as carbon sources and followed by the electrodeposition of PC for the first time on the RCDs/PGE surface to successfully produced a new support; PC/RCDs/PGE. NiNPs were then electroloaded on/in the PC/RCDs/PGE to produces the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE. The NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE was used as an electrocatalyst for the EOR in NaOH solution after being physicochemically characterized. According to the electrochemical results, the NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE electrocatalyst [Jp = 112 mA cm−2 and lower onset potential (Eonset) = 0.32 V] led to a notable enhancement of the electrocatalytic activity for EOR while compared to NiNPs/RCDs/PGE, NiNPs/PC/PGE, NiNPs/PGE, RCDs/PGE and unmodified PGE. Here, the notable electrocatalytic activity toward the EOR was noticed when the NiNPs were added to the PC/RCDs scaffold on the PGE. The obtained results further showed that NiNPs/PC/RCDs/PGE can acts as nickel-based electrocatalysts with good performance for additional uses in direct ethanol fuel cells. This combination leverages the high conductivity and dispersion properties of RCDs, as well as the stability of PC, to create a highly effective support for metallic nanoparticles electrocatalysts for EOR.

Biuck Habibi: Supervision, Monitoring, Editing, Discussing and Revising. Elnaz Minaie: All practical works in lab, Visualization, Investigation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing and Data curation. Khalil Farhadi: Supervision, Software and Validation.
